page: Google Cloud Platform c4d-highcpu-384 is a compute-optimized server instance belonging to the c4d family. It features 384 dedicated vCPUs built on the x86_64 CPU architecture. The instance is configured with 720 GB of system memory, yielding a memory-to-core ratio of approximately 1.88 GB per vCPU. This allocation prioritizes raw processing power over memory capacity, making it suitable for highly parallelized execution paths.

This server configuration does not include local NVMe storage or integrated GPU hardware, meaning all storage requirements must be met via network-attached block storage. Additionally, it does not provide complimentary public IPv4 addresses. The absence of bundled local storage and GPU accelerators reduces hardware overhead, focusing resources entirely on CPU execution.

From a qualitative cost-efficiency perspective, the c4d-highcpu-384 offers a high density of dedicated compute resources relative to its memory footprint. This profile is designed for CPU-bound workloads that do not require local SSD caching or graphics acceleration. Suitable workloads include large-scale computational simulations, high-performance computing tasks, heavy compilation pipelines, and CPU-intensive batch processing.
description: Google Cloud Platform c4d-highcpu-384 is a compute-optimized server instance within the c4d family, featuring 384 dedicated vCPUs on the x86_64 architecture and 720 GB of system memory. This configuration provides a resource ratio of 1.88 GB of RAM per vCPU, prioritizing raw processing power over memory capacity. The instance does not include local storage or GPU accelerators, and it offers zero complimentary public IPv4 addresses. Without local storage or GPU overhead, the resource density is optimized strictly for raw execution scaling. This hardware profile is qualitatively cost-efficient for highly parallel, CPU-bound tasks where local disk I/O and graphics acceleration are unnecessary. It is designed for workloads such as high-performance computing, large-scale batch processing, and intensive CPU-based simulations.
